items.front: 4-5-8: When should you inform an aircraft when to expect climb or descent clearance?
Phraseology to tell aircraft when to expect higher altitude at a fix
items.back: Expect higher at (fix). Or: Expect higher/lower in (number) miles/minutes.

items.front: What is the key condition for using a visual holding point?
items.back: The pilot must be familiar with the location and able to determine it by visual reference to the surface.

items.front: When must ATC approve a pilot-requested holding flight path deviation?
items.back: When obstacles and traffic conditions permit.

items.front: What special separation is required at an unmonitored NAVAID?
items.back: Separate holding aircraft from any other aircraft occupying the course the holding aircraft will follow if NAVAID signals are lost.

items.front: When must transfer of jurisdiction occur under 4-7-9?
items.back: Early enough to allow the receiving facility to clear the aircraft beyond the clearance limit before the aircraft reaches it.

items.front: What must be stated in an approach clearance to an uncontrolled airport?
items.back: The name of the airport. Example: 'Cleared ILS approach to Centerville airport.'

items.front: What must be included when relaying an approach clearance through a non-air-carrier station?
items.back: The weather report, when required and available. May be done by telling the station to issue current weather.

items.front: What altitudes may be specified for military high altitude instrument approaches for separation?
items.back: Altitudes above those shown on the high altitude instrument approach procedures chart.

items.front: How are maximum altitudes determined for military high altitude instrument approaches?
items.back: Through coordination between the ATC facility and the military authority to avoid rate-of-descent or airspeed limitations.
